title: Panhypopituitarism Meets Prostate Cancer: Profound Weakness After Stopping Testosterone
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: why 'normal' total T4/cortisol can mislead (free vs total, binding globulins and sex steroids), why central hypothyroidism is titrated to free T4 not TSH, how to judge glucocorticoid adequacy in ACTH deficiency, the can't-miss cord-compression red flag, and why testosterone cannot be replaced in active prostate cancer
authors: Resident/fellowship training module — illustrative teaching case

patient.chiefComplaint: Profound proximal muscle weakness and fatigue weeks after testosterone was stopped.
pmh: Panhypopituitarism after surgery for a pituitary macroadenoma
medications: Testosterone enanthate 200 mg IM every 2 weeks — recently discontinued by urology

caseDescription: A 68-year-old man with surgical panhypopituitarism, stable for years on levothyroxine, hydrocortisone and testosterone, is diagnosed with localized prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 3+4) and has his testosterone stopped (androgen deprivation). Six to eight weeks later he develops profound proximal muscle weakness (struggling to rise from a chair), fatigue and cold intolerance. Vitals are stable with no orthostasis; sodium 138, potassium 4.2, glucose 92, CBC normal; total T4 8.5 µg/dL ('normal') and an AM cortisol 18 µg/dL ('normal'). The dilemma the resident poses: why is he weak when 'everything is normal'?
keyFindings: Profound proximal weakness after androgen deprivation in a hypopituitary man — the cause is almost always multifactorial, not a single elegant mechanism
Total T4 and a random cortisol are unreliable here: central hypothyroidism is judged by FREE T4, and a cortisol on hydrocortisone reflects the dose taken, not adrenal adequacy
Sex steroids change thyroxine/cortisol binding globulins — withdrawing testosterone raises TBG and can transiently lower free T4 (a real but usually modest, self-limiting effect)
Loss of testosterone's anabolic effect (hypogonadal/ADT-related myopathy and fatigue) is the most certain contributor to the weakness
Can't-miss red flag in prostate cancer with new weakness: metastatic spinal-cord/cauda-equina compression and bone metastasis/hypercalcaemia — exclude before blaming hormones
Testosterone cannot be replaced during active prostate cancer; the hypogonadal symptoms must be managed without it
finalDiagnosis: Multifactorial weakness in a hypopituitary man after androgen deprivation for prostate cancer, in which the headline teaching trap is over-reliance on 'normal' TOTAL hormone levels. Practically: re-measure FREE T4 (central hypothyroidism is titrated to free T4 in the mid-upper reference range, never to TSH) and up-titrate levothyroxine if it is low — withdrawing testosterone raises thyroxine-binding globulin and can transiently reduce free T4; reassess glucocorticoid adequacy clinically (a random cortisol on hydrocortisone does not confirm sufficiency) and provide stress cover/empirical optimisation if adrenal under-replacement is suspected; and recognise that the loss of testosterone itself causes fatigue and proximal myopathy that cannot be reversed with androgen while the cancer is active. Before attributing the weakness to binding-globulin physiology, exclude the dangerous prostate-cancer causes of weakness — metastatic spinal-cord compression, bone metastases and hypercalcaemia. Testosterone replacement is withheld during active disease.
learningObjectives: Explain why total T4 and a random cortisol mislead after a change in sex-steroid status, and which free-hormone/clinical measures to use instead.

Describe how sex steroids alter TBG/CBG (estrogen raises them robustly; androgen withdrawal raises TBG more modestly) and the resulting transient free-hormone shifts in replacement-dependent patients.
Titrate central (secondary) hypothyroidism to free T4, not TSH, and judge glucocorticoid adequacy clinically with stress dosing rather than chasing assays.

Generate a complete, safety-first differential for profound weakness in prostate cancer — including metastatic cord compression, bone metastasis/hypercalcaemia, and ADT/hypogonadal myopathy — before settling on a binding-protein explanation.
State why testosterone is withheld in active prostate cancer and how to manage the hypopituitary axes through androgen deprivation.

items.title: Why a 'normal' total T4 and a 'normal' cortisol do not settle this case
items.content: The trap is treating total hormone levels as the answer. Only the free (unbound) fraction is biologically active, and assays of total T4 and total cortisol move with their binding proteins. Two specific reasons the quoted numbers are unreliable here: (1) Total T4 of 8.5 says nothing about the free T4 if thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG) has shifted — and in central hypothyroidism you manage by free T4 anyway, because TSH is uninterpretable. (2) The 'AM cortisol 18' is largely a measurement of the hydrocortisone he swallowed, not proof that his cortisol replacement is adequate — exogenous hydrocortisone is cortisol and is detected by the assay, so the value depends entirely on timing relative to the dose. So 'everything is normal' is an illusion created by the wrong tests; the work is to look at free hormones and clinical status.
keyPoints: Only free hormone is active; total assays track binding proteins
Central hypothyroidism is judged by free T4, not TSH/total T4
A cortisol drawn on hydrocortisone reflects the dose taken, not adrenal adequacy

items.title: How sex steroids move TBG and CBG — getting the direction and the caveats right
items.content: Sex steroids regulate hepatic binding-globulin synthesis, and the best-established effect is that ESTROGENS markedly raise thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG) and corticosteroid-binding globulin (CBG) — which is why starting oestrogen reliably increases levothyroxine requirements in hypothyroid women. ANDROGENS act in the opposite direction, lowering TBG (and SHBG); the androgen effect is real but generally smaller, and the effect on CBG/cortisol is less well established than on TBG. So withdrawing testosterone is expected to RAISE TBG, which in a patient on a fixed levothyroxine dose transiently lowers free T4 until a new steady state is reached — a plausible contributor to evolving hypothyroid symptoms over weeks. Two honesty checks for residents: the magnitude is usually modest and partly self-limiting (free T4 at steady state is set mainly by dose and clearance), and the parallel 'free cortisol falls because CBG rises' story is more speculative — do not assert it as firmly as the TBG effect.
keyPoints: Estrogen raises TBG/CBG robustly; androgen withdrawal raises TBG more modestly
Stopping testosterone ⇒ ↑TBG ⇒ transient ↓free T4 on a fixed LT4 dose
The CBG/free-cortisol limb is weaker evidence — flag it, don't assert it

items.title: Why central hypothyroidism is titrated to free T4 and never to TSH
items.content: In secondary (central) hypothyroidism the pituitary cannot generate an appropriate TSH, so TSH is low, normal or only mildly raised regardless of tissue thyroid status — using it to titrate is meaningless and dangerous. Replacement is titrated to the FREE T4, aiming for the mid-to-upper part of the reference range (free T3 is a secondary guide). That is exactly why this patient needs a free T4 measured now: if withdrawing testosterone has nudged TBG up and pulled free T4 down toward or below the lower range, his cold intolerance and sluggishness fit relative hypothyroidism, and the fix is to raise levothyroxine and re-check free T4 in 6 weeks. A reflexive 'TSH normal, thyroid fine' would miss it entirely.
keyPoints: TSH is uninterpretable in central hypothyroidism — do not titrate to it
Target free T4 in the mid-upper reference range
Measure free T4 here; raise LT4 if low and recheck at ~6 weeks

items.title: How to judge glucocorticoid adequacy in ACTH deficiency (and why an ACTH stim test is the wrong move here)
items.content: You cannot confirm that this man's hydrocortisone is sufficient from a single random cortisol, because the assay is reading his exogenous dose. Adequacy in established ACTH deficiency is judged clinically — energy, appetite, weight, blood pressure, sodium, and response to dose — and by ensuring the regimen mimics physiology (roughly 15–25 mg/day in divided doses, most in the morning). A short ACTH (Synacthen) stimulation test is also the wrong tool in this scenario: it probes adrenal reserve and is confounded by exogenous glucocorticoid, and the patient is on replacement regardless of the result. The safe, practical response to suspected under-replacement is to optimise the regimen, give explicit sick-day/stress-dose rules, and have a low threshold for parenteral hydrocortisone if he deteriorates — not to order a confounded stimulation test. Remember too the cardinal rule: in combined deficiency, glucocorticoid must be adequate before/with thyroid hormone, because correcting thyroid status raises cortisol demand and can precipitate adrenal crisis.
keyPoints: Random cortisol on hydrocortisone can't prove adequacy — judge clinically
ACTH stim test is confounded on steroids and doesn't change that he needs replacement
Ensure glucocorticoid cover before correcting thyroid; teach stress dosing

items.title: Why profound weakness in prostate cancer is a red flag until proven otherwise
items.content: Before attributing the weakness to elegant binding-protein physiology, exclude the dangerous causes that prostate cancer itself produces. Profound or progressive weakness — especially proximal/lower-limb, with any back pain, sensory level, bladder/bowel change or saddle anaesthesia — must trigger urgent evaluation for metastatic spinal-cord or cauda-equina compression, a neuro-oncological emergency where delay costs irreversible paraplegia; it needs urgent MRI and corticosteroids, not an endocrine clinic appointment. Bone metastases can also cause hypercalcaemia (weakness, confusion, polyuria) — though his calcium should be checked, it is not quoted here. The teaching discipline is sequencing: rule out cord compression and metabolic emergencies first, then refine the endocrine explanation. 'Other labs normal' does not exclude a structural cord lesion, which is a clinical and imaging diagnosis.
keyPoints: New/profound weakness + prostate cancer ⇒ exclude metastatic cord/cauda-equina compression urgently (MRI + steroids)
Check calcium — bone mets can cause hypercalcaemia
Normal routine labs do not exclude a structural cord lesion

items.title: Why the weakness is multifactorial — and which factor is most certain
items.content: Resist the single-mechanism story. The most certain contributor is the loss of testosterone itself: androgens are anabolic for muscle, and androgen deprivation reliably causes fatigue, reduced muscle mass and proximal weakness — well documented with ADT and entirely expected in a man who has just stopped replacement. Layered on top may be unmasked relative (central) hypothyroidism from the TBG shift (cold intolerance, slow-relaxing reflexes — confirm with free T4), and possible glucocorticoid under-replacement (fatigue, anorexia; no hypotension because secondary adrenal insufficiency spares aldosterone). Anaemia from androgen loss is real but develops over months, so it is unlikely to explain weakness at 6–8 weeks. And the can't-miss structural causes (cord compression, hypercalcaemia) sit above all of these in priority. Good practice names the whole differential and assigns likelihood, rather than crowning the most memorable mechanism.
keyPoints: Most certain: hypogonadal/ADT myopathy and fatigue from losing testosterone
Plausible add-ons: relative central hypothyroidism (free T4), glucocorticoid under-replacement
Anaemia takes months; structural causes (cord/hypercalcaemia) outrank all — exclude first

items.title: Why testosterone cannot simply be restarted to fix the weakness
items.content: The obvious 'cure' — give the testosterone back — is contraindicated while the prostate cancer is active, because androgens can drive prostate-cancer growth; this is the entire rationale for androgen deprivation. The 'saturation model' (the hypothesis that prostate androgen receptors saturate at low testosterone, so further testosterone may not stimulate growth) and emerging data on testosterone therapy in selected men after definitive treatment of low-risk disease are interesting but are NOT standard of care for active cancer and must not be used to justify replacing testosterone here. So the hypogonadal symptoms have to be managed without androgen: optimise the other axes (free-T4-guided levothyroxine, adequate glucocorticoid), resistance exercise and physiotherapy for muscle, bone protection, and clear counselling that some fatigue/strength loss is an expected, accepted trade-off of treating the cancer. Any reconsideration of testosterone is a future, oncology-led conversation, not an acute fix.
keyPoints: Testosterone is withheld in active prostate cancer — androgens can drive tumour growth
The saturation model / post-treatment TRT data are not standard of care for active disease
Manage hypogonadal symptoms without androgen; revisit only via oncology later

items.title: How androgen deprivation is actually delivered — and the flare to anticipate
items.content: Two practical points about the cancer treatment shape the endocrine picture. First, for localized intermediate-risk disease (Gleason 3+4) the primary treatment is usually definitive local therapy (radical prostatectomy or radiotherapy), with androgen deprivation used as an adjunct to radiotherapy rather than as lifelong castration for all — so the duration of the low-testosterone state, and thus of the binding-globulin shift, depends on the oncologic plan and is worth clarifying with urology. Second, if a GnRH (LHRH) agonist such as leuprolide is used, it causes an initial testosterone surge ('flare') before suppression, conventionally covered by a short course of an anti-androgen; GnRH antagonists and orchiectomy avoid the flare. Knowing the modality and timeline helps you predict how persistent the hormonal shifts will be and when to recheck free T4.
keyPoints: Localized Gleason 3+4: primary therapy is surgery/radiotherapy; ADT is often adjunctive, not necessarily lifelong
GnRH agonists cause an initial testosterone flare — cover with an anti-androgen
The ADT modality/duration sets how persistent the binding-globulin shift will be

items.title: Why changing one pituitary axis forces review of the others
items.content: Panhypopituitarism is an interdependent system, not a set of independent prescriptions. A change in the gonadal axis (here, stopping testosterone) perturbs binding globulins and therefore the apparent and free levels of thyroid and adrenal hormones; correcting thyroid status raises cortisol demand; growth-hormone status influences body composition and well-being. The operating rule is that any deliberate change in one axis mandates reassessment of the others — in this case, when testosterone was withdrawn, the team should have proactively planned a free-T4 check and a glucocorticoid-adequacy review rather than waiting for the patient to present weak. Building that reflex ('one axis changed → re-check the rest') is the durable lesson that outlasts the specific binding-globulin mechanism.
keyPoints: The pituitary axes interact — binding globulins, cortisol demand, body composition
Any planned change in one axis ⇒ proactively reassess the others
Withdrawing testosterone should have prompted a free-T4 + glucocorticoid review upfront

items.title: How to work up weakness after stopping testosterone in a hypopituitary man — a one-page rule
items.content: Algorithm: (1) Safety first — for profound/progressive weakness with any back pain, sensory level or sphincter change, urgently exclude metastatic spinal-cord/cauda-equina compression (MRI + corticosteroids); check calcium for hypercalcaemia. (2) Use the right thyroid test — measure FREE T4 (TSH is useless centrally); if low/low-normal, raise levothyroxine toward the mid-upper range and recheck at 6 weeks, recognising that stopping testosterone raises TBG. (3) Judge glucocorticoid adequacy clinically (not by a random cortisol drawn on hydrocortisone); optimise the regimen, reinforce sick-day/stress dosing, and have a low threshold for parenteral hydrocortisone — ensure steroid cover before correcting thyroid. (4) Attribute the residual weakness honestly to hypogonadal/ADT myopathy, which cannot be reversed with testosterone during active cancer; support with exercise/physiotherapy and bone protection. (5) Embed the panhypopituitarism rule — one axis changed, re-check the rest — and coordinate the ADT plan/timeline with urology.
keyPoints: Exclude cord compression/hypercalcaemia → measure free T4 (not TSH) → review glucocorticoid clinically
Up-titrate LT4 to mid-upper free T4; steroid cover before thyroid correction
Accept hypogonadal myopathy (no testosterone during active cancer); recheck the whole axis

items.question: Why can this patient be symptomatically hypothyroid and under-replaced despite a 'normal' total T4 and a 'normal' AM cortisol?
items.answer: Because both quoted tests measure the wrong thing for this situation. Only the free (unbound) hormone is active, and total assays move with their binding proteins. Stopping testosterone raises thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG), so a 'normal' total T4 can coexist with a free T4 that has fallen toward or below the lower limit — and in central hypothyroidism you must titrate to free T4 anyway because TSH is uninterpretable. The 'AM cortisol 18' is similarly misleading: it is largely a reading of the hydrocortisone he took (exogenous hydrocortisone is cortisol and is detected by the assay), so it reflects dose and timing, not whether his replacement is physiologically adequate. The correct assessment is a free T4 plus clinical judgement of glucocorticoid adequacy, not reassurance from total levels.
keyPoints: Total assays track binding proteins; only free hormone is active
↑TBG after stopping testosterone can lower free T4 with a 'normal' total T4
A cortisol drawn on hydrocortisone reflects the dose, not adrenal adequacy

items.question: How would you evaluate and manage this man's profound weakness, in priority order?
items.answer: Sequence by danger. (1) Exclude the prostate-cancer emergencies: with profound/progressive weakness, any back pain, sensory level or bladder/bowel change, urgently image the spine (MRI) for metastatic cord/cauda-equina compression and start corticosteroids if confirmed; check serum calcium for hypercalcaemia. (2) Re-test the endocrine axes correctly: measure FREE T4 (not TSH) — if low or low-normal, increase levothyroxine toward the mid-upper reference range and recheck at ~6 weeks, knowing that withdrawing testosterone raises TBG. (3) Reassess glucocorticoid adequacy clinically rather than by a random cortisol; optimise the hydrocortisone regimen, reinforce sick-day/stress-dose rules, and ensure glucocorticoid cover before correcting thyroid status. (4) Attribute residual weakness to hypogonadal/ADT myopathy, which cannot be reversed with testosterone while the cancer is active — support with resistance exercise/physiotherapy and bone protection. (5) Coordinate the ADT modality and duration with urology and apply the panhypopituitarism rule of re-checking every axis after any change.
keyPoints: First exclude cord compression/hypercalcaemia (MRI, calcium)
Then free T4-guided LT4 up-titration; clinical glucocorticoid review with stress cover
Accept and support hypogonadal myopathy; no testosterone during active cancer

items.stem: A 68-year-old hypopituitary man on levothyroxine, hydrocortisone and testosterone has testosterone stopped for prostate cancer, then becomes weak and cold-intolerant. Total T4 is 8.5 µg/dL ('normal') and AM cortisol 18 µg/dL ('normal'). What is the best next thyroid test?
options: Free T4 — central hypothyroidism is titrated to free T4, and a binding-globulin shift can lower it despite a normal total T4
TSH — to confirm the thyroid axis is adequately replaced
Thyroid uptake scan
Total T3 only
items.explanation: In central (secondary) hypothyroidism TSH is uninterpretable, so replacement is judged by free T4 (target mid-upper range). Stopping testosterone raises TBG, which can lower free T4 even with a normal total T4 — hence free T4 is the right test. An uptake scan and an isolated total T3 do not address replacement adequacy.

items.stem: Which statement about sex steroids and binding globulins is most accurate?
options: Estrogens robustly raise TBG/CBG; androgen withdrawal raises TBG more modestly, transiently lowering free T4 on a fixed levothyroxine dose
Androgens raise TBG, so stopping testosterone lowers TBG and raises free T4
Binding globulins have no effect on thyroid hormone delivery
Only liver disease, never hormones, changes binding-globulin levels
items.explanation: Estrogens markedly increase TBG and CBG (raising levothyroxine requirements when started); androgens lower TBG, so withdrawing testosterone raises TBG and can transiently reduce free T4 in a replacement-dependent patient. The effect is real but usually modest and partly self-limiting, and the parallel CBG/cortisol effect is less well established.

items.stem: Before attributing this man's profound weakness to binding-globulin physiology, which diagnosis must be urgently excluded?
options: Metastatic spinal-cord / cauda-equina compression from prostate cancer
Subclinical hyperthyroidism
Benign essential tremor
Allergic rhinitis
items.explanation: Profound or progressive weakness in a man with prostate cancer is a red flag for metastatic spinal-cord or cauda-equina compression — a neuro-oncological emergency requiring urgent MRI and corticosteroids. Normal routine labs do not exclude a structural cord lesion. The other options are not emergencies and do not fit.

items.stem: How should glucocorticoid adequacy be assessed in this man already taking hydrocortisone?
options: Clinically (energy, appetite, weight, BP, sodium, response to dose), with stress-dose education — a random cortisol on hydrocortisone does not confirm adequacy
By a single random cortisol, which reliably confirms replacement is sufficient
By a short ACTH stimulation test performed while continuing hydrocortisone
By measuring TSH
items.explanation: A cortisol drawn on hydrocortisone reflects the dose taken, not adrenal adequacy, and an ACTH stimulation test is confounded by exogenous steroid (and the patient needs replacement regardless). Adequacy in established ACTH deficiency is judged clinically, with optimisation and stress-dose rules. TSH is irrelevant to the adrenal axis.

items.stem: The patient asks to restart testosterone because he feels weak. What is the appropriate response?
options: Testosterone is withheld during active prostate cancer; manage hypogonadal symptoms without androgen and optimise the other axes
Restart full testosterone immediately to relieve the weakness
Restart a low dose of testosterone as the saturation model proves it is safe in active cancer
Replace testosterone with an anabolic steroid instead
items.explanation: Androgens can drive prostate-cancer growth, which is the basis of androgen deprivation, so testosterone is withheld during active disease. The saturation model and post-treatment TRT data are not standard of care for active cancer. Symptoms are managed by optimising thyroid/adrenal replacement, exercise/physiotherapy and bone protection — not by restarting androgen or an anabolic steroid.

items.stem: Which is the MOST certain contributor to proximal weakness at 6–8 weeks after stopping long-term testosterone in this patient?
options: Loss of testosterone's anabolic effect (hypogonadal/ADT-related myopathy and fatigue)
Anaemia from loss of testosterone's erythropoietic drive
Acute hyperkalaemic paralysis
Levothyroxine overdose
items.explanation: Androgen deprivation reliably causes fatigue, reduced muscle mass and proximal weakness, so losing testosterone is the most certain contributor at this timeframe. Androgen-loss anaemia develops over months; potassium is normal (4.2); and the picture is of hypothyroid-type symptoms, not thyroxine excess.

items.statement: In central (secondary) hypothyroidism, levothyroxine should be titrated to normalise the TSH.
items.explanation: False. The pituitary cannot mount an appropriate TSH in central hypothyroidism, so TSH is uninterpretable. Replacement is titrated to the free T4 (mid-upper reference range), with free T3 as a secondary guide.

items.statement: Starting estrogen raises thyroxine-binding globulin and typically increases levothyroxine requirements in hypothyroid patients.
items.explanation: True. Estrogens increase TBG (and CBG), raising total T4 and increasing levothyroxine requirements — the well-established counterpart to the more modest effect of androgen withdrawal raising TBG seen in this case.

items.assertion: A normal total T4 reliably excludes hypothyroidism in this patient after testosterone withdrawal.
items.reason: Only the free fraction of thyroxine is biologically active, and total T4 varies with thyroxine-binding globulin.
items.explanation: The assertion is false but the reason is true. Because only free thyroxine is active and total T4 moves with TBG — which rises when testosterone is withdrawn — a normal total T4 does NOT exclude a low free T4 and relative hypothyroidism. The reason correctly explains why the assertion fails. Hence option 3.

items.assertion: In a hypopituitary patient with combined deficiencies, glucocorticoid replacement should be adequate before thyroid hormone is increased.
items.reason: Raising thyroid hormone increases cortisol turnover and metabolic demand and can precipitate an adrenal crisis if glucocorticoid is insufficient.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because correcting thyroid status increases cortisol demand and clearance, ensuring adequate glucocorticoid first prevents precipitating adrenal crisis when levothyroxine is increased. Hence option 0.

items.question: Explain the binding-globulin mechanism by which stopping testosterone can unmask relative hypothyroidism, and state one important caveat.
items.answer: Androgens lower thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG); withdrawing testosterone therefore raises TBG, increasing the bound fraction of thyroxine. In a patient on a fixed levothyroxine dose this transiently lowers the free (active) T4 until a new steady state is reached, which can produce hypothyroid symptoms (cold intolerance, sluggishness) despite a 'normal' total T4 — and since this is central hypothyroidism, free T4, not TSH, must be used to detect and correct it. Caveat: the androgen effect on TBG is generally modest and partly self-limiting (steady-state free T4 is set mainly by dose and clearance), and the analogous CBG/free-cortisol effect is less well established — so the weakness is usually multifactorial rather than explained by this mechanism alone.

items.question: List, in priority order, the causes of profound weakness to consider in this patient.
items.answer: (1) Metastatic spinal-cord/cauda-equina compression from prostate cancer — a neuro-oncological emergency to exclude first (MRI, corticosteroids). (2) Hypercalcaemia from bone metastases (check calcium). (3) Hypogonadal/ADT-related myopathy and fatigue from loss of testosterone — the most certain endocrine contributor at 6–8 weeks. (4) Unmasked relative (central) hypothyroidism from the TBG shift — confirm with free T4. (5) Glucocorticoid under-replacement (fatigue, anorexia; no hypotension as aldosterone is spared in secondary adrenal insufficiency). (6) Anaemia from androgen loss — real but usually develops over months, so less likely this early.

items.question: What general panhypopituitarism principle does this case illustrate, and how should it have been applied when testosterone was stopped?
items.answer: That the pituitary axes are interdependent, so any deliberate change in one axis mandates reassessment of the others — through shared binding globulins, altered cortisol demand and changes in body composition/well-being. Applied here, when urology stopped testosterone the team should have proactively planned a free-T4 check (anticipating the rise in TBG and a possible need to up-titrate levothyroxine) and a clinical review of glucocorticoid adequacy with reinforced stress-dose rules, rather than waiting for the patient to return with profound weakness. The durable reflex is: one axis changed, re-check the rest.

items.stem: A patient is symptomatically hypothyroid and under-replaced despite a 'normal' total T4 and a 'normal' AM cortisol. What is the most likely explanation?
options: A) Total T4 and cortisol assays are not reflective of free hormone levels or adrenal function in this context
B) The patient has a pituitary tumor causing TSH resistance
C) The patient is taking excessive levothyroxine leading to iatrogenic hyperthyroidism
D) The patient has a genetic defect in thyroid hormone receptor binding
items.explanation: Total T4 assays correlate with binding proteins (e.g., TBG), not free (active) T4. Stopping testosterone increases TBG, lowering free T4 despite normal total T4. AM cortisol reflects exogenous hydrocortisone dose, not adrenal reserve. Free T4 and clinical assessment are required for accurate replacement titration.

items.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with profound weakness. What is the priority order for evaluation and management?
options: A) Check TSH, then MRI for spinal cord compression, then adjust glucocorticoids
B) Image spine for cord compression/hypercalcaemia, then assess free T4, then review glucocorticoid regimen
C) Initiate testosterone replacement, then check calcium, then optimize hydrocortisone
D) Perform MRI, then check TSH, then start resistance exercise
items.explanation: Urgent exclusion of spinal cord compression (via MRI) and hypercalcaemia (via calcium check) is priority. Free T4 assessment and glucocorticoid review follow, as per the stepwise approach.

items.stem: Which of the following is the most priority cause of profound weakness to consider in this patient?
options: A) Metastatic spinal-cord/cauda-equina compression from prostate cancer
B) Hypercalcaemia from bone metastases
C) Hypogonadal/ADT-related myopathy and fatigue
D) Unmasked relative (central) hypothyroidism
items.explanation: Metastatic spinal-cord/cauda-equina compression is a neuro-oncological emergency requiring immediate evaluation (e.g., MRI, corticosteroids), making it the highest priority. Other options are important but lower priority in acute presentation.
